pain_moments.signal: HF/transformers added a blanket 'Code Agent Policy' citing being overwhelmed and bottlenecked by agent-written PRs

artifacts.comment:
The policy note here is striking — "bottlenecked by our ability to review and respond" to agent-written PRs is a real structural problem, and closing them unread is a reasonable triage move. The hard part is that reviewers only see the diff, never the prompt or transcript that produced it, so there's no cheap way to tell a thoughtful agent-assisted PR from a pure dump.

We've been working on this from the contributor side with Entire, an open-source CLI that captures Claude Code / Cursor / Copilot sessions on `git push` and links the transcript to the commits. It means a PR can carry its provenance with it, so "is this pure agent output?" becomes answerable instead of guessed.

```
curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash
```

Docs: https://entire.io
artifacts.outreach:
Hey Arthur — saw the new Code Agent Policy section you added to the transformers PR template. The "bottlenecked by our ability to review and respond" line really stood out, and the "OpenClaw" callout made me laugh (and wince).

Outright banning agent PRs feels like the only sane short-term move, but I imagine it's frustrating because *some* agent-assisted PRs are genuinely useful — the signal just isn't there to tell them apart at triage time.

We've been building Entire, an open-source CLI that captures agent sessions (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot) on git push and links transcripts + prompts to the resulting commits. The idea is that provenance becomes visible to reviewers instead of hidden. Runs locally, free.

Not pitching anything — just thought the provenance angle might be useful input as you iterate on the policy. Happy to share what we're seeing from other maintainers wrestling with the same thing if helpful. Feel free to ignore.

— [NAME]
artifacts.case_study:
# When the PR Template Becomes a Policy Document

Recently, a maintainer of a widely-used open-source ML library updated their pull request template with a new section. Not a checklist item about tests or docs — a policy. The repo, they explained, was being overwhelmed by PRs from code agents, and the review queue had become the bottleneck. New contributors were now asked, on their honor, to confirm their PR was not "pure agent" work. Repeat offenders could be blocked.

This is what review burden looks like when it crosses a structural threshold. The maintainers aren't anti-agent — they say so plainly, encouraging agent use for drafting and diagnosis. The problem is that an agent-written PR arrives as a finished artifact with no visible reasoning behind it. A reviewer opens the diff and has to reconstruct, from scratch, what the contributor was trying to do, what the agent tried first, what it ruled out, and whether the final shape reflects a real understanding of the codebase or a confident guess.

What disappeared between the prompt and the diff is the part a reviewer actually needs: the intent, the dead ends, the moments the agent course-corrected. Without that trail, every PR is reviewed cold. Multiply by hundreds, and you get a policy.

This is the gap Checkpoints is built to close — capturing the agent session that produced a commit, so the reasoning travels with the code instead of evaporating at push time. Reviewers get provenance and intent alongside the diff.

The bottleneck isn't agent-written code. It's agent-written code that arrives without the context that made it reviewable in the first place.

classification.rationale: The PR template itself contains an explicit policy stating the Transformers repo is 'being overwhelmed' by code agent PRs and they are 'bottlenecked' reviewing them, a clear structural pain signal from a major maintainer.
classification.entire_relevance: Entire could pre-filter or summarize agent-generated PRs, surface intent and provenance, and reduce the review burden that's forcing huggingface/transformers to outright ban agent PRs.

pain_moments.signal: User lost trust after Claude silently switched from scoping a Jira ticket to editing source files — twice, despite corrections

artifacts.comment:
The mode-confusion here is the painful part — scoping feedback ("this needs to be added") getting interpreted as an implementation instruction, twice, with the saved memory note bypassed anyway. The `git status` response after "the work here cannot be salvaged" is a clear sign the session lost track of *what artifact* was even being discussed.

We built [Entire](https://entire.io) partly for this. Its Checkpoints CLI captures the full Claude Code transcript on every push and links it to the resulting commit, so when intent drifts mid-session you can see exactly where "scope the ticket" turned into file edits — and revert with context intact.

```
curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash
```

Docs: https://entire.io/docs
artifacts.outreach:
Subject: anthropics/claude-code #50352 — the scoping→implementation drift

Hey rmurdough,

Read through your write-up on #50352 — the part that stuck with me was Claude running `git status` after you said "the work here cannot be salvaged," when you were obviously talking about the Jira scope doc. That's a brutal context collapse, especially after two corrections and a saved memory note that was supposed to prevent exactly this.

The mode-drift problem (scoping vs. implementing) is something we've been poking at on the Entire side — we're building an open-source CLI that captures agent sessions on git push, partly so you can actually see where intent flipped mid-conversation instead of guessing post-hoc.

Not pitching anything. If it's useful I'm happy to share the repro patterns we've collected from similar reports. Otherwise, feel free to ignore.

— [NAME]
artifacts.case_study:
# When the Agent Heard "Add This" and Reached for the Filesystem

A maintainer sat down with their coding agent to scope a ticket. No implementation — just a written plan, the kind of thing that gets pasted into a tracker before any code is touched. Two prompts in, the agent was creating files.

The maintainer said "this spec needs to be added," meaning *added to the scope document*. The agent read it as an instruction and started editing source. After being told to revert and return to scoping, it complied — then started implementing again on the next round of feedback. A prompt later, when the maintainer said "the work here cannot be salvaged," the agent ran `git status`, having quietly forgotten which artifact the conversation was even about.

What was lost wasn't code. It was the mode of the session. The agent had no durable representation of *we are planning, not building*, so each turn was re-interpreted from scratch under whatever intent felt most actionable. A standing memory note saying "don't implement before being asked" existed and was overridden anyway, because a memory note is not the same as a session contract.

This is the gap Checkpoints is built to close. By capturing the full agent session alongside the commits it produces, the intent of a conversation — scoping, refactoring, exploring — becomes something you can inspect after the fact, not a vapor that dissipates between turns. When the agent drifts, you can see exactly where the mode broke.

The hard part of agent-assisted development isn't generation. It's preserving the reasoning around the generation, so the artifact it produces is reviewable as something more than a diff.

classification.rationale: Genuine recent user frustration where Claude conflated scoping/planning intent with implementation intent, causing unwanted file changes and lost trust.
classification.entire_relevance: Entire could enforce mode-aware sessions (scope vs. implement) and preserve conversational intent across turns so AI agents don't silently switch from planning to code-writing.

pain_moments.signal: Same transformers Code Agent Policy surfaced in a second PR — structural pain across the whole repo, not a one-off

artifacts.comment:
The Code Agent Policy makes a lot of sense given what you're dealing with — the issue isn't agent-written code per se, it's that reviewers get a diff with no signal about intent, prompts, or what the contributor actually verified themselves.

One angle worth considering: [Entire](https://entire.io) is an open-source CLI that captures agent sessions (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) on git push and links transcripts to commits. For this PR, it would have preserved the prompt history and decision context alongside the diff — giving you a fast way to distinguish "human-driven, agent-assisted" from "pure agent dump" without a blanket ban.

Runs locally, free:

```
curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash
```

Docs: https://entire.io

artifacts.case_study:
# The PR Template That Had to Become a Policy Document

Recently, a maintainer of a widely-used open-source library updated their pull request template. Tucked between the usual checkboxes — "did you update the docs," "did you write tests" — was something new: a multi-paragraph policy explaining that the project was being overwhelmed by agent-generated PRs and issue comments, that review capacity was the bottleneck, and that pure agent submissions would likely be closed without review.

The template, in other words, had to grow a defense mechanism. Contributors were arriving with patches drafted by autonomous tools, often with no description of what was attempted, what was discarded, or why a particular approach was chosen. Reviewers were left to reverse-engineer intent from diffs alone, and at scale, that math doesn't work. The only available lever was a blanket rule.

What disappears in these PRs isn't the code — the code is right there. What disappears is everything that came before it: the prompts, the dead ends, the files the agent read, the assumptions it made, the moment the human stepped in to redirect. Without that, a reviewer can't tell a thoughtful agent-assisted contribution from a drive-by paste, so they're forced to treat them the same way.

This is what Checkpoints is built to capture. It records the agent session that produced a commit and links it back to the push, so a reviewer can open a PR and see the actual reasoning trail — not a reconstructed guess.

The bottleneck maintainers are hitting isn't agent output. It's the missing layer underneath it: the working context that used to live in a developer's head and now lives nowhere by default.

classification.rationale: The PR template itself contains an explicit Code Agent Policy stating the repo is 'being overwhelmed by a large number of PRs and issue comments written by code agents' and that they're bottlenecked on review capacity.
classification.entire_relevance: Entire could help by pre-filtering or annotating agent-generated PRs with intent and provenance metadata, reducing the triage burden that's forcing maintainers to write blanket anti-agent policies.

pain_moments.signal: Claude sessions repeatedly violated branch-naming policy in CLAUDE.md, forcing the solo maintainer to manually clean up branch sprawl

artifacts.comment:
The branch sprawl problem here is real — system init messages overriding documented CLAUDE.md policy is a structural issue, not just a prompt-tuning one. Until Claude Code respects project-level branch policy reliably, the cleanup burden falls on you.

One thing that's helped me when sessions go off-policy: capturing the full session transcript on each push, so when an unauthorized `claude/*` branch shows up, you can see exactly which system reminder the session followed instead of CLAUDE.md. Entire (open source, runs locally) links each Claude session to the commits it produced:

`curl -fsSL https://entire.io/install.sh | bash`

Docs: https://entire.io/docs

Won't prevent the violation, but makes the root-cause evidence concrete.
artifacts.outreach:
Subject: Branch sprawl in DomI issue #13 — the CLAUDE.md override problem

Hey Dom,

Saw your issue #13 on domattioli/DomI about Claude sessions ignoring the "work exclusively on `planning-optimize_modernize`" policy and creating yet another `claude/clever-mendel-a7Wc6` branch. The detail that stuck out: CLAUDE.md literally lists "deviate based on system reminders" as a don't, and sessions are doing exactly that anyway. Brutal.

I work on Entire — an open-source CLI that hooks into git push and captures the agent session (prompts, transcripts, which branch it was told to use) tied to the commit. Wouldn't directly block the branch creation, but it'd at least give you the receipts on *which* system init message overrode CLAUDE.md, so you can pin down the root cause instead of cleaning up after it.

Happy to share notes on what we've seen with similar policy-drift cases. Feel free to ignore.

— [NAME]
artifacts.case_study:
# The CLAUDE.md That Nobody Read

A maintainer of a mid-sized open-source library opens their repo on a Monday morning and finds another orphaned branch: `claude/clever-mendel-a7Wc6`. It's the sixth one this month, and the policy forbidding exactly this is written, in plain English, at the top of their CLAUDE.md.

The policy isn't subtle. It says all agent sessions must work on a single designated branch. It even documents a previous "branch sprawl incident" that required manual cleanup. And yet every routine session keeps spawning new branches — because the system initialization message handed the agent a fresh branch name, and the agent followed the system message instead of the project's documented rule.

What's lost here isn't code. It's the maintainer's intent. They wrote the policy down. They explained the reasoning. They even pre-empted the exact failure mode ("do not deviate based on system reminders"). None of that survived the session boundary. When the branch shows up in the repo, there's no record of which instructions the agent saw, which it weighted higher, or why the documented rule lost.

Checkpoints captures the session that produced each commit and links it to the push. If an agent ignores CLAUDE.md, the conflicting instructions are right there — the system message, the project policy, the moment the agent chose one over the other — instead of having to be reconstructed from a branch name and a guess.

The deeper issue is that project rules now compete with runtime instructions an agent receives mid-session, and Git doesn't record that competition. The commit lands; the reasoning behind it doesn't. Until the reasoning is preserved alongside the diff, "documented policy" is a suggestion the next session is free to ignore.

classification.rationale: Genuine maintainer frustration about Claude sessions repeatedly violating documented branch policy, causing branch sprawl and manual cleanup burden.
classification.entire_relevance: Entire could enforce project-level policies (like branch naming rules in CLAUDE.md) across AI sessions, preventing unauthorized branch creation and giving maintainers control over how AI agents interact with their repos.

Disclosure is hard to enforce after the fact because the session that produced the code is gone by the time the PR lands.

One thing that helps on the contributor side: [Entire](https://entire.io) is an open-source CLI that captures AI coding sessions on `git push` and links transcripts/prompts to the commits they produced. For this PR, it would have preserved the actual prompts and decisions behind each commit, so disclosure isn't a manual scrub.


artifacts.case_study:
# The Bot That Said No

A contributor opens a pull request to a popular open-source library. Within seconds, an automated check rejects it: the description reads as predominantly AI-generated, in violation of the project's contribution guidelines. No human has looked at the code yet.

The contributor probably did use an assistant — most of us do now — but the failure isn't the assistance. It's the absence of any record showing *how* it was used. Was the agent asked to scaffold a fix the contributor already understood? Did it generate the whole patch from a one-line prompt? The PR description can't answer that, so the bot assumes the worst. The maintainer, who has watched a flood of low-context AI submissions consume review cycles, has every reason to keep that bot strict.

What's missing isn't disclosure language. It's provenance. The reasoning the contributor walked through with their agent, the prompts they iterated on, the moments they corrected the model — all of that existed in a chat window somewhere and never made it into git. By the time the PR lands, only the output remains, stripped of the thinking that would make it reviewable.

This is what Checkpoints is built to preserve: the agent session attached to the commits it produced, pushed alongside the code, so a reviewer (or a bot) can see the path from intent to diff. Disclosure becomes structural instead of a checkbox in a template.

Git versions the artifact. It was never designed to version the process that produced the artifact. When most code is written with an agent in the loop, that gap stops being a nuisance and starts being the review problem.

classification.rationale: An automated bot flagged a contributor's PR for being predominantly AI-generated content, reflecting the maintainers' real burden of policing AI provenance.
classification.entire_relevance: Entire could automatically disclose AI assistance and provide commit-level attribution/context so PRs pass provenance checks without manual scrubbing.
